executiveSummary:
## Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

**Enrollment and Spending Trends**

National Medicaid enrollment reached 75.3 million beneficiaries as of January 2026, with most major states experiencing enrollment declines following the end of pandemic-era continuous enrollment provisions. California and Illinois saw the steepest decreases at -6.2% year-over-year, while Michigan declined -7.6%. Texas, with 4.0 million enrollees, experienced a moderate -3.1% decline, outperforming California and Illinois but trailing North Carolina, which posted the only growth at +0.5% among major states. National per-enrollee spending averaged $2,869 in fiscal year 2024, with significant state variation ranging from Florida's efficient $2,031 per enrollee to Pennsylvania's $4,377. Texas spent $2,988 per enrollee, positioning the state slightly above the national average but well below high-cost states like Pennsylvania ($4,377), Massachusetts ($4,201), and Ohio ($3,561).

**Quality Performance and State Comparisons**

Quality measure performance reveals substantial geographic disparities, with the national average at 65.6 out of 100 points. New England and Western states dominate high performance, led by Massachusetts (81), Minnesota (80), and Vermont (79), while Southern states consistently underperform, with Mississippi (48), Oklahoma (51), and Alabama (52) ranking lowest. Texas scored 62 points, ranking 30th nationally in the "moderate" performance tier—trailing peer states like North Carolina but outperforming several Southern neighbors. Notably, Texas maintains high managed care penetration at 93.3%, covering 3.9 million enrollees and exceeding the national average of 83.2%, suggesting strong infrastructure for coordinated care delivery. Recent federal regulatory activity indicates heightened focus on interoperability standards and prior authorization processes, while ongoing OIG oversight emphasizes the need for continued attention to program integrity across all state programs.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, trailing only California and New York. Despite its substantial size, Texas demonstrates relatively efficient cost management with per-enrollee expenditures of $2,988—4.2% higher than the national average of $2,869 but significantly lower than expansion states like Pennsylvania ($4,377) and Ohio ($3,561). The state's -3.1% year-over-year enrollment decline aligns closely with the national trend as states unwind pandemic-era continuous coverage policies, performing better than large peers like Illinois (-6.2%) and Michigan (-7.6%). Texas has embraced managed care more extensively than most states, with 93.3% penetration compared to the national average of 83.2%, suggesting a strong commitment to coordinated care delivery models that can drive both cost efficiency and care coordination.

However, Texas faces significant quality challenges that demand attention. The state's quality score of 62/100 ranks 30th nationally and falls into the "moderate" performance tier—a concerning position given its substantial resources and infrastructure. While Texas outperforms deep-south non-expansion peers like Alabama (52) and Mississippi (48), it lags behind expansion states and even some southern states like North Carolina, which achieved modest enrollment growth (+0.5%) and lower per-enrollee costs ($2,616). The combination of non-expansion status limiting coverage for low-income adults, coupled with middling quality performance, suggests Texas may be missing opportunities to leverage its scale and managed care infrastructure for better outcomes. Key areas to monitor include whether the state can translate its high managed care penetration into measurable quality improvements and how effectively it manages the ongoing enrollment transitions while maintaining access for its most vulnerable populations.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost control issues.

alerts.title: Five States Show Critically Low Quality Performance
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi all scored below 55 on quality measures, well under the national average of 65.6.

alerts.title: Massachusetts Combines High Quality with High Spending
alerts.description: Massachusetts ranks in top 5 for quality (81/100) but also has high per-enrollee costs at $4,201, suggesting premium care at premium prices.

alerts.title: Florida Shows Unusually Low Per-Enrollee Spending
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below the national average, which may indicate underfunding or efficient care delivery.

alerts.title: No States Show Enrollment Growth Above 1%
alerts.description: All major states except North Carolina show enrollment declines, with only NC showing modest growth of 0.5% year-over-year.

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score ranks #30/50, below national average
executiveInsights.summary: Texas scored 62/100 on quality measures, ranking 30th nationally and falling below the national average of 65.6. This places Texas in the 'moderate' tier, well below high-performing states like MA (81) and MN (80).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality performance directly impacts contract renewals and CMS oversight intensity. Poor quality scores can trigger federal intervention and affect Accenture's deliverables.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement initiatives to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs $119 above national average at $2,988
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee compared to the national average of $2,869, representing a 4.2% premium. With 4M+ enrollees, this translates to roughly $480M in excess annual spending.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-enrollee costs suggest potential inefficiencies that Accenture could address through cost management initiatives, creating clear value propositions for expanded services.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost optimization opportunities with HHSC finance team

executiveInsights.title: New federal interoperability and prior auth rules issued April 2026
executiveInsights.summary: CMS published new Medicare and Medicaid interoperability standards and prior authorization requirements in the Federal Register on April 14, 2026.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: These regulatory changes will require system updates and compliance work that Accenture can support, representing potential contract expansions or modifications.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review new interoperability requirements impact on current deliverables

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment declined 3.1% YoY to 4.03M members
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped from approximately 4.16M to 4.03M enrollees, a 3.1% year-over-year decline that aligns with national unwinding trends.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment declines may reduce contract values and change operational requirements, requiring Accenture to adjust staffing and deliverable scopes accordingly.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag enrollment impact on contract scope to program management

executiveInsights.title: Texas managed care penetration 93.3% exceeds national average of 83.2%
executiveInsights.summary: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3.86M MC enrollees, significantly above the national average of 83.2%. This represents one of the highest MC adoption rates nationally.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration indicates sophisticated program administration needs that align with Accenture's capabilities, supporting arguments for expanded technical services.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review managed care optimization opportunities with HHSC

riskOpportunity.label: Texas Managed Care Recompete
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's 93.3% managed care penetration creates major procurement opportunities as contracts come up for renewal.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor HHSC procurement portal and position for managed care organization technology and operations support opportunities.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality Measure Gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks #30 nationally with a 62/100 quality score, indicating significant improvement needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop quality improvement solutions targeting Texas's specific performance gaps to reach top-tier status.

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment Decline Impact
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% YoY, potentially reducing program scope and budgets.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Pivot service offerings toward efficiency and cost optimization to align with shrinking enrollment base.

riskOpportunity.label: Federal Interoperability Mandate
riskOpportunity.description: New CMS interoperability and prior authorization standards create compliance requirements for state systems.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Build specialized interoperability consulting practice to help Texas HHSC meet federal compliance deadlines.

riskOpportunity.label: HHS OIG Oversight Risk
riskOpportunity.description: Active OIG oversight creates audit and compliance risks for current and future Texas Medicaid operations.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring and documentation practices across all Texas HHSC engagements.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-Enrollee Cost Efficiency
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's $2,988 per-enrollee cost sits above efficient states like Florida ($2,031), creating optimization pressure.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop cost reduction strategies benchmarked against high-performing, low-cost states.

riskOpportunity.label: Multi-State Competitive Expansion
riskOpportunity.description: Success in Texas positions Accenture for similar opportunities in other large Medicaid states facing similar challenges.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop replicable service offerings that can scale to other large states with similar managed care penetration.

riskOpportunity.label: CMS Data Collection Changes
riskOpportunity.description: Proposed changes to CMS information collection activities may alter reporting requirements and system needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor proposed rule changes and prepare system modification capabilities for new reporting requirements.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 4,032,864 Medicaid enrollees as of January 2026.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee annually in fiscal year 2024.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 managed care enrollees.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas scored 62 out of 100 on quality measures, ranking 30th of 50 states.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two Texas procurement portals are actively monitoring opportunities.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Five active regulatory and oversight items are currently in progress.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific milestone date is available in the current data.
